decks.caption:
I missed the devserver workflow for personal projects.

So I built eternalMac around the Mac laptop + Mac Mini setup I wanted:

- persistent shell sessions
- synced files
- private reachability
- local hardware
- less laptop state

Anyone else using a Mini this way?

slides.alt: EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 1: I turned my Mac Mini into a personal devserver.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 2: Close the laptop. The shell keeps running.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 3: Reconnect from another network.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 4: Files and selected agent config stay in sync.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 5: This should be normal solo-builder infrastructure.

decks.caption:
I kept seeing "loops" described like a concept.

I wanted the control pieces packaged into something I could actually run on local repos overnight.

Omnius is my attempt at that control layer.

What part of agent loops still feels too hand-rolled to you?

slides.alt: Omnius Instagram carousel slide 1: Everyone talks about AI agent loops.
Omnius Instagram carousel slide 2: A useful coding loop is not one prompt.
Omnius Instagram carousel slide 3: Queue. Preflight. Isolated worker.
Omnius Instagram carousel slide 4: That is what I built Omnius for.
Omnius Instagram carousel slide 5: Local-first agent infrastructure is getting real.

decks.caption:
The more I use AI coding tools, the more I care about context continuity.

Shell access is easy.

Keeping the right project + agent state across machines is the part that gets annoying.

That is why I added Dotsync to eternalMac.

slides.alt: EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 1: The annoying part is not SSH. It is context.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 2: Your agent config lives on one machine.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 3: EternalMac now supports Dotsync.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 4: ~/.claude and ~/.codex can follow the session.
EternalMac Instagram carousel slide 5: Same project. Same agent context. Different device.

decks.caption:
I built AgentAirlift because serious coding sessions should have an exit path.

It does not preserve hidden model state.

It does preserve the observable session trail: messages, tool calls, repo snapshot, warnings, and handoff docs.

That is enough to make switching agents much less chaotic.

slides.alt: AgentAirlift Instagram carousel slide 1: AI coding sessions should not be trapped in one tool.
AgentAirlift Instagram carousel slide 2: I want an explicit handoff, not a prayer.
AgentAirlift Instagram carousel slide 3: AgentAirlift turns a session into a transfer bundle.
AgentAirlift Instagram carousel slide 4: It does not claim hidden model-state magic.
AgentAirlift Instagram carousel slide 5: Portable sessions are basic agent infrastructure.

decks.caption:
The first time a long agent run dies at the quota wall, it feels like a product problem.

After it happens a few times, it is clearly an ops problem.

QuotaSentry is a small local guardrail for that.

What is the most annoying failure mode in your agent workflow?

slides.alt: QuotaSentry Instagram carousel slide 1: The quota wall is an infrastructure problem.
QuotaSentry Instagram carousel slide 2: The run should not die because the reset window is close.
QuotaSentry Instagram carousel slide 3: Detect. Wait. Resume.
QuotaSentry Instagram carousel slide 4: That is why I built QuotaSentry.
QuotaSentry Instagram carousel slide 5: AI coding needs ops guardrails.

decks.caption:
I do not think the next jump in AI coding comes only from better prompts.

The missing layer is operating context:

where the session runs, how it survives, how it syncs, how it pauses, how it hands off, and how a human reviews it.

That is the thread connecting the tools I have been building.

slides.alt: Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 1: Solo dev infrastructure is changing fast.
Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 2: Code generation is only one layer.
Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 3: Machines. Sessions. Sync. Quota.
Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 4: EternalMac: local devserver. Omnius: local loops.
Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 5: AgentAirlift: portable sessions. QuotaSentry: guardrails.
Local-first AI dev infrastructure Instagram carousel slide 6: The future is better operating context.
